annotations.label: Von Neumann algebra (W*-algebra)

anchor.snippet: a von Neumann algebra or W*-algebra is a *-algebra of bounded operators

annotations.note: VonNeumannAlgebra is a *-closed subalgebra of H →L[ℂ] H equal to its double commutant; WStarAlgebra gives the abstract version.

annotations.label: Double commutant theorem (informal)

anchor.snippet: His double commutant theorem shows that the analytic definition is equivalent

annotations.note: Commutant infrastructure exists and S'' = S holds by definition, but the equivalence with weak-closedness (the actual theorem) is an explicit TODO in the file.

annotations.label: Essentially bounded functions on the real line

anchor.snippet: of essentially bounded measurable functions on the real line is a commutative von Neumann algebra

annotations.note: L∞ exists as MeasureTheory.Lp but is not endowed with or characterized as a von Neumann algebra.

annotations.label: All bounded operators on a Hilbert space

anchor.snippet: of all bounded operators on a Hilbert space

annotations.note: B(H) = H →L[ℂ] H has a CStarAlgebra instance, but it is not established as a von Neumann algebra (no ⊤ : VonNeumannAlgebra H or WStarAlgebra instance exists).

annotations.label: First definition: weakly closed *-algebra

anchor.snippet: The first and most common way is to define them as weakly closed *-algebras of bounded operators

annotations.note: Mathlib defines VonNeumannAlgebra via the double commutant, not via weak-operator-topology closure, which is not formalized.

annotations.label: Norm-closed *-algebras are C*-algebras

anchor.snippet: The *-algebras of bounded operators that are closed in the norm topology are C*-algebras

annotations.note: Any StarSubalgebra of a CStarRing inherits the C⋆-identity, but the completeness/closedness packaging into a full CStarAlgebra is not stated.

annotations.label: Second definition: double commutant

anchor.snippet: The second definition is that a von Neumann algebra is a subalgebra of the bounded operators closed under involution

annotations.note: This is exactly Mathlib's definition: a StarSubalgebra of H →L[ℂ] H with centralizer_centralizer' giving S'' = S.

annotations.label: Von Neumann double commutant theorem

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann double commutant theorem ( von Neumann 1930 ) says that the first two definitions are equivalent.

annotations.note: Commutant machinery is present, but the theorem that weak closure equals the double commutant is explicitly noted as not yet proven.

annotations.label: Abstract definition via predual

anchor.snippet: Sakai (1971) showed that von Neumann algebras can also be defined abstractly as C*-algebras that have a predual

annotations.note: WStarAlgebra is defined as a CStarAlgebra possessing a Banach space predual, following Sakai.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of the predual

anchor.snippet: The predual of a von Neumann algebra is in fact unique up to isomorphism.

annotations.note: WStarAlgebra only asserts existence of a predual; uniqueness up to isomorphism is not formalized.

annotations.label: Factor

anchor.snippet: A factor is a von Neumann algebra with trivial center

annotations.note: No notion of factor (von Neumann algebra with trivial center) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: A finite von Neumann algebra is one which is the direct integral of finite factors

annotations.note: Direct integral decomposition and finiteness for von Neumann algebras are absent.

annotations.label: Properly infinite von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: properly infinite von Neumann algebras are the direct integral of properly infinite factors

annotations.note: Proper infiniteness of von Neumann algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Separable von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: A von Neumann algebra that acts on a separable Hilbert space is called separable

annotations.note: No separability notion for von Neumann algebras exists.

annotations.label: Generated von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann algebra generated by a set of bounded operators on a Hilbert space is the smallest von Neumann algebra containing all those operators

annotations.note: There is no lattice/closure or generation operation for VonNeumannAlgebra.

annotations.label: Tensor product of von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two von Neumann algebras acting on two Hilbert spaces is defined to be the von Neumann algebra generated by their algebraic tensor product

annotations.note: Von Neumann algebra tensor products are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Von Neumann algebras are semihereditary

anchor.snippet: Von Neumann algebras are semihereditary : every finitely generated submodule of a projective module is itself projective

annotations.note: No result on semiheredity of von Neumann algebras exists.

annotations.label: Affiliated operators form a von Neumann regular ring

anchor.snippet: The *-algebra of affiliated operators of a finite von Neumann algebra is a von Neumann regular ring

annotations.note: Affiliated operators are not defined and no von Neumann regular ring notion is present.

annotations.label: Classification of commutative von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: Every commutative von Neumann algebra is isomorphic to L ∞ ( X ) for some measure space

annotations.note: Mathlib has Gelfand duality for commutative C⋆-algebras but not the L∞(X) classification of commutative von Neumann algebras.

annotations.label: Projection

anchor.snippet: Operators E in a von Neumann algebra for which E = EE = E* are called projections

annotations.note: IsStarProjection is defined as a self-adjoint idempotent (p*p = p, star p = p) in any star ring.

annotations.label: Subspace belonging to M

anchor.snippet: A subspace of the Hilbert space H is said to belong to the von Neumann algebra M if it is the image of some projection in M

annotations.note: The notion of a subspace belonging to a von Neumann algebra is not formalized.

annotations.label: 1:1 correspondence projections/subspaces

anchor.snippet: This establishes a 1:1 correspondence between projections of M and subspaces that belong to M

annotations.note: The general correspondence between orthogonal/star projections and closed subspaces exists, but the von Neumann algebra-relative version (projections of M ↔ subspaces belonging to M) does not.

annotations.label: Closures of images and kernels belong to M

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that the closure of the image of any operator in M and the kernel of any operator in M belongs to M

annotations.note: This closure/kernel membership statement for von Neumann algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Murray–von Neumann equivalence

anchor.snippet: Two subspaces belonging to M are called ( Murray–von Neumann ) equivalent if there is a partial isometry mapping the first isomorphically onto the other

annotations.note: Murray–von Neumann equivalence of projections via partial isometries is not formalized.

annotations.label: Additivity of equivalence

anchor.snippet: The equivalence relation ~ thus defined is additive in the following sense

annotations.note: Additivity of Murray–von Neumann equivalence is not present (the equivalence itself is undefined).

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein theorem for operator algebras

anchor.snippet: The Schröder–Bernstein theorems for operator algebras gives a sufficient condition for Murray-von Neumann equivalence

annotations.note: The operator-algebra Schröder–Bernstein theorem for projections is not formalized.

annotations.label: Total order on equivalence classes in a factor

anchor.snippet: If M is a factor, ≤ is a total order on equivalence classes of projections

annotations.note: Comparison theory of projections in a factor is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite projection

anchor.snippet: E is said to be a finite projection if there is no projection F < E

annotations.note: Finite projections (in the Murray–von Neumann sense) are not defined.

annotations.label: Von Neumann algebra generated by projections

anchor.snippet: a von Neumann algebra is generated by its projections; this is a consequence of the spectral theorem

annotations.note: This generation result (only the finite/compact spectral theorem exists) is not formalized for von Neumann algebras.

annotations.label: Projections of a finite factor form a continuous geometry

anchor.snippet: The projections of a finite factor form a continuous geometry .

annotations.note: Continuous geometries and finite factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Factor (center is scalar multiples)

anchor.snippet: A von Neumann algebra N whose center consists only of multiples of the identity operator is called a factor

annotations.note: Although Subalgebra.center exists, factors (trivial-center von Neumann algebras) are not defined.

annotations.label: Direct integral decomposition into factors

anchor.snippet: every von Neumann algebra on a separable Hilbert space is isomorphic to a direct integral of factors

annotations.note: Direct integral decomposition into factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Every factor has one of three types

anchor.snippet: Murray & von Neumann (1936) showed that every factor has one of 3 types as described below

annotations.note: The type I/II/III classification of factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unique decomposition into types I, II, III

anchor.snippet: Every von Neumann algebra can be written uniquely as a sum of von Neumann algebras of types I, II, and III

annotations.note: The type decomposition of von Neumann algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Discrete and continuous factors

anchor.snippet: A factor is called discrete (or occasionally tame ) if it has type I, and continuous

annotations.note: Discrete/continuous classification of factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Semifinite and purely infinite factors

anchor.snippet: A factor is called semifinite if it has type I or II, and purely infinite if it has type III

annotations.note: Semifinite/purely infinite factor terminology is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite and properly infinite factors

anchor.snippet: A factor is called finite if the projection 1 is finite and properly infinite otherwise

annotations.note: Finiteness/proper infiniteness of factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type I factor

anchor.snippet: A factor is said to be of type I if there is a minimal projection E ≠ 0

annotations.note: Type I factors and minimal projections are not formalized.

annotations.label: Type I factors are B(H)

anchor.snippet: Any factor of type I is isomorphic to the von Neumann algebra of all bounded operators on some Hilbert space

annotations.note: The classification of type I factors as B(H) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type II factor

anchor.snippet: A factor is said to be of type II if there are no minimal projections but there are non-zero finite projections

annotations.note: Type II factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Type II_1 and II_∞

anchor.snippet: If the identity operator in a type II factor is finite, the factor is said to be of type II 1

annotations.note: Type II_1 and II_∞ distinctions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of hyperfinite type II factors

anchor.snippet: These are the unique hyperfinite factors of types II 1 and II ∞

annotations.note: Hyperfinite factors and their uniqueness are not formalized.

annotations.label: Unique tracial state on a type II_1 factor

anchor.snippet: Murray & von Neumann (1937) proved the fundamental result that a factor of type II 1 has a unique finite tracial state

annotations.note: Tracial states and the uniqueness on type II_1 factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Semifinite trace on type II_∞ factor

anchor.snippet: A factor of type II ∞ has a semifinite trace, unique up to rescaling

annotations.note: Semifinite traces on type II_∞ factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Fundamental group of a type II_∞ factor

anchor.snippet: is called the fundamental group of the type II ∞ factor

annotations.note: The fundamental group of a factor is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tensor product yields type II_∞

anchor.snippet: The tensor product of a factor of type II 1 and an infinite type I factor has type II ∞

annotations.note: Type computations of tensor products of factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Fundamental group of a type II_1 factor

anchor.snippet: The fundamental group of a type II 1 factor is defined to be the fundamental group of its tensor product

annotations.note: The fundamental group of a type II_1 factor is not formalized.

annotations.label: Connes: property (T) gives countable fundamental group

anchor.snippet: Connes then showed that the von Neumann group algebra of a countable discrete group with Kazhdan's property (T)

annotations.note: This result of Connes is not formalized.

annotations.label: Popa: trivial fundamental group

anchor.snippet: Sorin Popa showed that the fundamental group can be trivial for certain groups

annotations.note: Popa's trivial fundamental group result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type II_1 group von Neumann algebra (ICC group)

anchor.snippet: An example of a type II 1 factor is the group von Neumann algebra of a countable infinite discrete group

annotations.note: Group von Neumann algebras and ICC examples are not formalized.

annotations.label: McDuff: uncountably many type II_1 factors

anchor.snippet: McDuff (1969) found an uncountable family of such groups with non-isomorphic group von Neumann algebras

annotations.note: McDuff's family of type II_1 factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type III factor

anchor.snippet: type III factors are factors that do not contain any nonzero finite projections at all

annotations.note: Type III factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Type III_λ via Connes spectrum

anchor.snippet: if the Connes spectrum (of its modular group) is 1 then the factor is of type III 0

annotations.note: Connes spectrum, modular groups, and type III_λ subclassification are not formalized.

annotations.label: Trace and projections on type III factors

anchor.snippet: The only trace on type III factors takes value ∞ on all non-zero positive elements

annotations.note: Traces on type III factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Type III as crossed product (Tomita–Takesaki)

anchor.snippet: any type III factor can be written in a canonical way as the crossed product of a type II ∞ factor and the real numbers

annotations.note: The Tomita–Takesaki crossed product structure theorem is not formalized (only an early StandardSubspace toward modular theory exists).

annotations.label: Predual

anchor.snippet: Any von Neumann algebra M has a predual M ∗ , which is the Banach space of all ultraweakly continuous linear functionals on M

annotations.note: WStarAlgebra.exists_predual asserts an abstract predual, but the concrete M_* as the space of ultraweakly continuous functionals is not constructed.

annotations.label: M is the dual of its predual

anchor.snippet: As the name suggests, M is (as a Banach space) the dual of its predual

annotations.note: WStarAlgebra builds in an isometric isomorphism StrongDual ℂ X ≃ₗᵢ⋆ M, capturing 'M is the dual of its predual' for the asserted predual.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of the predual

anchor.snippet: The predual is unique in the sense that any other Banach space whose dual is M is canonically isomorphic to M ∗

annotations.note: Uniqueness of the predual is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sakai: predual characterizes von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: Sakai (1971) showed that the existence of a predual characterizes von Neumann algebras among C* algebras

annotations.note: Sakai's predual criterion is taken as the definition of WStarAlgebra, but its equivalence with the concrete VonNeumannAlgebra is not proven.

annotations.label: Predual is a proper closed subspace of the dual

anchor.snippet: The predual M ∗ is a closed subspace of the dual M* (which consists of all norm-continuous linear functionals on M ) but is generally smaller

annotations.note: The predual as a closed subspace of the dual is not formalized.

annotations.label: Predual of L∞(R) is L1(R)

anchor.snippet: The predual of the von Neumann algebra L ∞ ( R ) of essentially bounded functions on R is the Banach space L 1 ( R ) of integrable functions

annotations.note: Lp duality exists in part, but the predual-of-L∞ statement framed for von Neumann algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Predual of B(H) is trace class operators

anchor.snippet: The predual of the von Neumann algebra B ( H ) of bounded operators on a Hilbert space H is the Banach space of all trace class operators

annotations.note: Trace-class operators (Schatten classes) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Predual of VN(G) is the Fourier algebra

anchor.snippet: The predual of the group von Neumann algebra VN ( G ) associated to the left regular representation of a locally compact group G is the Fourier algebra A ( G )

annotations.note: Neither the group von Neumann algebra nor the Fourier algebra is formalized.

annotations.label: Weight

anchor.snippet: A weight ω on a von Neumann algebra is a linear map from the set of positive elements

annotations.note: Weights on (von Neumann/C⋆-)algebras are not defined.

annotations.label: Positive linear functional

anchor.snippet: A positive linear functional is a weight with ω(1) finite

annotations.note: Positive linear maps (and the C⋆ specialization →ₚ[ℂ]) are defined, of which positive linear functionals A →ₚ[ℂ] ℂ are the special case, but the weight framework is absent.

annotations.label: State

anchor.snippet: A state is a weight with ω(1) = 1.

annotations.note: No normalized-state (ω(1) = 1) structure is defined for C⋆/von Neumann algebras.

annotations.label: Trace

anchor.snippet: A trace is a weight with ω( aa* ) = ω( a*a ) for all a .

annotations.note: Traces as tracial weights on operator algebras are not defined.

annotations.label: Tracial state

anchor.snippet: A tracial state is a trace with ω(1) = 1.

annotations.note: Tracial states are not defined.

annotations.label: Unique trace on a factor

anchor.snippet: Any factor has a trace such that the trace of a non-zero projection is non-zero

annotations.note: Existence/uniqueness of traces on factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Normal state from a unit vector (GNS)

anchor.snippet: then the functional a → ( av , v ) is a normal state

annotations.note: The GNS construction from a positive functional is formalized, but the vector-state functional a ↦ ⟨av, v⟩ and its normality are not stated.

annotations.label: Classification of modules by M-dimension

anchor.snippet: every such module H can be given an M -dimension

annotations.note: M-dimension of modules over a factor is not formalized.

annotations.label: Standard module

anchor.snippet: A module is called standard if it has a cyclic separating vector

annotations.note: Cyclic and separating notions are formalized for standard subspaces (toward Tomita–Takesaki), but standard modules over a factor are not defined.

annotations.label: Existence and uniqueness of standard representation

anchor.snippet: Each factor has a standard representation, which is unique up to isomorphism

annotations.note: Standard representations of factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalent characterizations of amenability

anchor.snippet: Connes (1976) and others proved that the following conditions on a von Neumann algebra M on a separable Hilbert space H are all equivalent

annotations.note: Amenability/injectivity of von Neumann algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Classification of amenable factors

anchor.snippet: The amenable factors have been classified: there is a unique one of each of the types

annotations.note: Connes' classification of amenable factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Amenable factors via group-measure space construction

anchor.snippet: All amenable factors can be constructed using the group-measure space construction of Murray and von Neumann

annotations.note: The group-measure space construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finiteness of tensor products

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two finite algebras is finite, and the tensor product of an infinite algebra and a non-zero algebra is infinite

annotations.note: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras and their finiteness are not formalized.

annotations.label: Type of a tensor product is the maximum

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The type of the tensor product of two von Neumann algebras (I, II, or III) is the maximum of their types

annotations.note: Type behavior under tensor products is not formalized.

annotations.label: Commutation theorem for tensor products

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The commutation theorem for tensor products states that

annotations.note: The commutation theorem for tensor products is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinite tensor product via states

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: von Neumann (1938) showed that one should choose a state on each of the von Neumann algebras

annotations.note: Infinite tensor products with respect to states are not formalized.

annotations.label: Araki–Woods (ITPFI) factors

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: Araki & Woods (1968) studied the case where all the factors are finite matrix algebras

annotations.note: Araki–Woods (ITPFI) factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Powers factors

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: Powers (1967) found an uncountable family of non-isomorphic hyperfinite type III λ factors for 0 < λ < 1, called Powers factors

annotations.note: Powers factors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Hyperfinite (not III_0) factors are Araki–Woods

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: All hyperfinite von Neumann algebras not of type III 0 are isomorphic to Araki–Woods factors

annotations.note: This classification of hyperfinite factors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bimodule (correspondence)

anchor.snippet: A bimodule (or correspondence) is a Hilbert space H with module actions of two commuting von Neumann algebras

annotations.note: Hilbert bimodules/correspondences over von Neumann algebras are not formalized.

annotations.label: Bimodules yield subfactors

anchor.snippet: Any bimodule over two factors always gives a subfactor since one of the factors is always contained in the commutant of the other

annotations.note: Subfactors from bimodules are not formalized.

annotations.label: Voiculescu: disjointness of non-amenable factor classes

anchor.snippet: Voiculescu has shown that the class of non-amenable factors coming from the group-measure space construction is disjoint from the class coming from group von Neumann algebras of free groups

annotations.note: Voiculescu's free-probability disjointness result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ozawa: hyperbolic groups give prime II_1 factors

anchor.snippet: Narutaka Ozawa proved that group von Neumann algebras of hyperbolic groups yield prime type II 1 factors

annotations.note: Ozawa's primeness theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Essentially bounded functions on a σ-finite measure space

anchor.snippet: The essentially bounded functions on a σ-finite measure space form a commutative (type I 1 ) von Neumann algebra acting on the L 2 functions

annotations.note: L∞ acting on L² is not formalized as a von Neumann algebra.

annotations.label: Kaplansky density theorem

anchor.snippet: A fundamental approximation theorem can be represented by the Kaplansky density theorem .

annotations.note: The Kaplansky density theorem for von Neumann algebras is not in Mathlib (the Kaplansky hits are unrelated ring/cardinality results).

annotations.label: Bounded operators on a Hilbert space (type I factor)

anchor.snippet: The bounded operators on any Hilbert space form a von Neumann algebra, indeed a factor, of type I

annotations.note: B(H) is a CStarAlgebra in Mathlib, but it is not established as a (type I factor) von Neumann algebra.

annotations.label: Commutant of a unitary group representation

anchor.snippet: the bounded operators commuting with G form a von Neumann algebra G ′

annotations.note: The commutant of a VonNeumannAlgebra is a VonNeumannAlgebra, but that the commutant of an arbitrary unitary group representation is a von Neumann algebra is not stated.

annotations.label: Von Neumann group algebra of a discrete group

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann group algebra of a discrete group G is the algebra of all bounded operators

annotations.note: Group von Neumann algebras are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tensor product as a von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two von Neumann algebras, or of a countable number with states, is a von Neumann algebra

annotations.note: Von Neumann algebra tensor products are not formalized.

annotations.label: Crossed product by a group

anchor.snippet: The crossed product of a von Neumann algebra by a discrete (or more generally locally compact) group can be defined, and is a von Neumann algebra

annotations.note: Crossed products of von Neumann algebras are not formalized.

annotations.label: Von Neumann algebras of equivalence relations and groupoids

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann algebras of a measurable equivalence relation and a measurable groupoid can be defined

annotations.note: Von Neumann algebras of equivalence relations/groupoids are not formalized.
